text: The surgeon positions the trocars for laparoscopic access to the abdomen, beginning with the lowering of the hepatic angle of the colon. Extreme cauterization is performed to highlight the inferior vena cava, the confluence with the right renal vein, and the aorta. The cauterization extends to the left, reaching the peritoneum and releasing the duodenum. The procedure continues at the level of the common hepatic artery with ganglionary curation, followed by similar actions at the gastro-duodenal artery.

The surgeon sections the distal part of the liver, facilitating access to the pancreas. The transverse mesocolon is lifted near the confluence of Henle, gradually exposing the superior mesenteric vein. An ultrasonic scalpel is used to perform a distant pancreatic section, ensuring no oncological compromise. The VIRSUNG duct, measuring three millimeters, is tutored using a percutaneous approach.

The surgeon sections the first range of the trites and uses it to control the gastro-duodenal artery, which is then sectioned between security clips. The release of the right edge of the superior mesenteric vein and the portal vein begins, with delicate traction applied medially towards the right lateral side. Collateral branches of the portal vein are identified and sectioned using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon releases the superior mesenteric vein and the portal vein from the small pancreas without using clips. The back portal blade is removed from the upper mesenteric artery. A clamp is placed on the gallbladder, which is then sectioned. The gallbladder is dissected and sectioned at the hepatic canal, avoiding the choledochal canal. The operation concludes with a cholecystectomy, removing the tips of the titer drain.

The surgeon navigates through the transmesogonic approach to reach a jejunal aneurysm. The large epiplomb is positioned in front of the vessels. A lateral terminal pancreato-jejunostomy is performed using barbed wire, completing the posterior pancreato-jejunostomy in total plan.
The surgeon uses laparoscopy to support the Virson canals, utilizing wires that can be stretched as needed without assistance.

The surgeon completes the posterior wall of the anastomosis. An anterotomy is performed using a monopolar bisturi, and the drenthutter is passed into the jejunum. The anterior plan is initiated using the same suture technique, securing the Virsum canals to the jejunum.

The surgeon completes the suture, preparing to move on to the gynecological hepatitis procedure, which will be performed 20 to 25 centimeters further using a PDS 4-0 suture. The first posterior sutures are done in reverse, followed by anterior sutures, with a lost drain in place.

The surgeon, positioned between the patient's legs, completes the posterior plan and performs an anterotomy. A lost drain is placed to support the anastomosis, and the anterior plan is executed similarly, completing the total plan.

The surgeon uses a linear clamp to perform a wide lateral anastomosis for gastrointestinal gestation, approximately seventy centimeters from the previous anastomoses, facilitating stomach emptying. The anterotomy orifices are closed using barbed wire sutures.
